claim: MAR classifies lost dog temperament into three types — gregarious (found close to home, often informally adopted), aloof (travel long distances, found weeks/months later, require patient food-based luring), xenophobic (farthest travel, highest car-strike risk, require traps or calm dogs) — each requiring a fundamentally different search strategy.
scope_conditions: MAR (Missing Animal Response) framework by Kat Albrecht; basis is extensive field case experience, not controlled study
quoted_support: MAR classifies dog temperament into three types: Gregarious dogs exhibit friendlier lost house dog behavior... Aloof dogs would be wary of strangers and are ones most likely to travel long distances... The most fearful type of dogs are classified as xenophobic... dogs with xenophobia will travel the farthest and because of their fearful behavior, they are also the most likely to be hit by a moving vehicle.

claim: 86-97% of lost dogs are eventually found, suggesting most lost dogs survive and are recoverable with appropriate methodology.
scope_conditions: PawMaw article citing survival rate studies; no specific study named in this article
quoted_support: 86% to 97% of lost dogs are eventually found, and this high percentage closely reflects a strong survival rate.

claim: Survival mode can activate instantly upon getting lost or may take weeks to develop; the sooner the search begins, the more likely the dog has not yet entered survival mode and will respond normally to owner.
scope_conditions: MAR framework; Kat Albrecht; field experience
quoted_support: Lost dogs may enter survival mode instantly while for some, it may take weeks before it kicks in. So, what does this tell you? The sooner you start looking for your dog, the more likely that he hasn't been in survival mode yet.
